coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of novel g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs heterojunction photocatalyst with enhanced visible light photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jiamei
Deng, Qiangyi
Yang, Ziyin
Zhang, Hongmei
Zhang, Conglu
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a high-efficient visible light responsive g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs Z-type heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully prepared by a two-step method for the photodegradation of organic pollutants in water. The combination of g-C3N4, CaTiO3 and CQDs could produce synergistic effect, which facilitated the separation of carriers, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic kinetic removal rate of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs for methylene blue could reach about 8 times of g-C3N4 and 5.5 times higher than that of CaTiO3. The effects of co-existing ions, initial pH, water source, and humic acid on meloxicam degradation were examined. Repeatability experiments indicated that the composite had good reusability and stability with 81.1 % removal for meloxicam after four cycles. The main active substances of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs in free radical capture experiment was ⋅O2 −. Possible degradation pathways of meloxicam were also analyzed. The results highlight the g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs Z-type heterojunction is a desirable photocatalyst for removing organic pollutants in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
Carbon quantum dots
CaTiO3
Heterojunction
Photodegradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of preparation conditions on g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs properties

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Young and Middle-aged Key Talent Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education of Liaoning Province
Foundation of Liaoning Province Education Administration

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Fund Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province ( LJKZ0910 ) and the Young and Middle-aged Key Talent Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( ZQN2021026 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB by g-C3N4/CQDs with different CQDs loading; (b) dynamic fitting curves; (c) kinetic rate constanst
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB by g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs with different CaTiO3 contents; (b) dynamic fitting curves; (c) kinetic rate constants.
(a) Degradation of MB by different photocatalysts under visible light; (b) dynamic fitting curves; (c) kinetic rate constants.
XRD patterns of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs, g-C3N4 and CaTiO3.
(a) SEM of g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs; (b) SEM of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs; (c) element distribution image of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs; (d) elemental mapping.
TEM of CQDs (a) and g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs (b).
(a) UV–vis DRS; (b) the band gap energies of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs and g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs.
(a) Effect of pH on MLX degradation; (b) zeta potential of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs; Effect of (c) catalyst dosage, (d) co-existing anions, (e) HA, (f) different water matrices on MLX degradation.
(a) Recyclability of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQD; (b) XRD patterns of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQD before and after photocatalysis.
Proposed photocatalytic degradation pathways of MLX.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of novel g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs heterojunction photocatalyst with enhanced visible light photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Benxi, Liaoning Province, 117004, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Benxi, Liaoning Province, 117004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a high-efficient visible light responsive g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs Z-type heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully prepared by a two-step method for the photodegradation of organic pollutants in water. The combination of g-C3N4, CaTiO3 and CQDs could produce synergistic effect, which facilitated the separation of carriers, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic kinetic removal rate of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs for methylene blue could reach about 8 times of g-C3N4 and 5.5 times higher than that of CaTiO3. The effects of co-existing ions, initial pH, water source, and humic acid on meloxicam degradation were examined. Repeatability experiments indicated that the composite had good reusability and stability with 81.1 % removal for meloxicam after four cycles. The main active substances of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs in free radical capture experiment was ⋅O2 −. Possible degradation pathways of meloxicam were also analyzed. The results highlight the g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs Z-type heterojunction is a desirable photocatalyst for removing organic pollutants in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater from domestic, agricultural, pharmaceutical industries and other industrial areas contains a large amount of organic pollutants that are difficult to degrade, such as dyes and drugs. These pollutants can harm the ecosystem and cause various effects on human body such as carcinogenesis, teratogenesis, mutagenesis and endocrine disruption. Traditional treatment methods such as adsorption [1], coagulation [2], and biochemical methods [3], with the shortcomings of low degradation efficiency, secondary pollution and so on [4]. Therefore, the search for simple and efficient methods to remove organic pollutants from water is an important issue. Semiconductor photocatalysis technology can convert solar energy into chemical energy to mineralize the difficult degradable organic pollutants into carbon dioxide and water. It has the advantages of high efficiency, simplicity and environmental protection and is an effective means of wastewater purification [5]. Graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4), is a typical two-dimensional non-metallic polymeric semiconductor material，which can be synthesized from precursors such as cyanamide, melamine, thiourea, and urea [6]. g-C3N4 has the nontoxicity, high photochemical stability and suitable band edge positions. The valence band (VB) level and conduction band (CB) level of g-C3N4 are calculated to be 1.73 eV and −0.97 eV, respectively [7]. g-C3N4 shows promising applications in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [8,9]. However, the photoactivity performance of pure g-C3N4 is low due to its high charge carriers recombination, low absorption coefficient, and low specific surface area [10]. In order to overcome the shortcomings of g-C3N4, many approaches have been studied, including morphology control [11,12], elemental doping [13,14] and construction of composite materials [15].

The construction of composite semiconductors to form heterogeneous structures and improve interfacial charge separation is an effective technique to improve photocatalytic performance [16]. For example, Ehsan's team [17] synthesized ZnSe/g-C3N4 composite for efficient degradation of Congo red dye. Wang et al. [18] prepared TiO2-modified g-C3N4, which could promote the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes and significantly enhance the photoreduction yield of CO2. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new fluorescent nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm and have a typical structure of graphitic carbon with graphene (sp2 carbon) or diamond-like (sp3 hybridized carbon) [19]. CQDs have the high electron transfer and light trapping abilities. In addition, the unique surface defect-induced upconversion photoluminescence effect of CQDs can convert UV light to visible light, thereby improving the light absorption performance of semiconductor catalysts [20,21]. So far, many photocatalysts have been developed with CQD as the active component, such as CdS/CQD [22], TiO2/CQD [23] and Bi2WO6/CQD [24] for the degradation of organic pollutants under the simulated sunlight. Thus, CQDs can be expected to enhance the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4.

Although there are many reports on g-C3N4 based binary composite photocatalysts, their utilization of photons and photocatalytic activity are still unsatisfactory. Therefore, ternary systems have attracted attention. For example, Liu et al. [25] prepared visible-light-driven Z-type g-C3N4/Bi2WO6/rGO heterojunction, and the large contact area between the heterojunction could reduce the combination of electron-hole pairs and effectively improve the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of ibuprofen. CaTiO3 is a typical ABO3 chalcogenide oxide with a stable crystal structure and excellent electronic configuration. It's energy band structure matches well with that of g-C3N4 [26,27], which can be directly compounded to form heterojunction photocatalytic system.

Based on the above findings, this study prepared CaTiO3/g-C3N4/CQD ternary composite photocatalyst by loading CQD on the surface of g-C3N4 and then compounding it with CaTiO3 by high temperature calcination method. The structure and properties of the CaTiO3/g-C3N4/CQD were systematically characterized. Meloxicam (MLX) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug of the enolformamide class widely used for its analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects [28]. Usually, this drug is incompletely metabolized in humans and animals and is excreted in urine and feces and enters the sewage network. Meloxicam has been detected in many aquatic systems because of their persistent and long-term biological activity [29]. The photocatalytic properties of the prepared CaTiO3/g-C3N4/CQD were investigated under simulated sunlight irradiation using MLX as a model pollutant. The effects of initial pH, catalyst dosing, co-existing inorganic ions, natural organic matter and different water bodies on the catalytic reaction were systematically investigated. In addition, the photocatalytic enhancement mechanism and the intermediate products produced during MLX degradation were investigated.

Calcium nitrate ((Ca(NO3)2⋅4H2O), 99 %) was supplied by Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Company offered titanium butoxide (Ti(OC4H9)4, 99 %), humic acid (HA, 99.0 %) and methylene blue (MB, 99 %). Polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG-200), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99.0 %), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 %) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Plant. Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company supplied melamine (99 %). Citric acid (CA), ammonium acetate, sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.0 %) and tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) were obtained from Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Company. Meloxicam (MLX, 99 %) was procured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All chemicals used were of analytical grade without further purified. Deionized water was used during experimental work.

3 g of citric acid was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water, and then the solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, followed by pH adjustment to 7.0 with the NaOH solution at a concentration of 1 mol L−1 to obtain CQDs solution. The above solution was freeze-dried to obtain CQDs powder. Finally, the powder was dispersed in deionized water to obtain a 1 mg mL−1 solution of CQDs, which was placed in a refrigerator (4 °C) and set aside.

The CaTiO3 was prepared according to our previous study [30]. In detail, 0.7085 g Ca(NO3)2⋅4H2O was dissolved in 59 mL PEG-200 by ultrasonication. 0.99 mL Ti(OC4H9)4 was dropwise added into the above solution and stirred until uniform. Next, 66 mmol of NaOH was introduced into the mixture. Then the obtained solution was heated at 180 °C for 24 h in a 100 mL autoclave. Finally, CaTiO3 powder was obtained after rinsing several times successively with acetone, dilute acetic acid, and deionized water and further drying at 60 °C.

The g-C3N4 was prepared according to previous study [30]. 10 g of melamine was placed in a tube furnace, followed by heating at 520 °C for 4 h in an air atmosphere. At last, the yellow product was obtained after natural cooling.

Ternary composites of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs were synthesized by a two-step method. First, the g-C3N4/CQDs material was prepared by a simple chemical impregnation method. 100 mg of g-C3N4 was added to 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol, followed by the CQDs solution and sonicated for 30 min. Then, the mixture was violently stirred for 12 h to make the CQDs adhere uniformly to the g-C3N4. The obtained g-C3N4/CQDs powers were collected after vacuum drying overnight. Finally, the obtained g-C3N4/CQDs and CaTiO3 were ground and mixed well, and then heated at 500 °C for 60 min and the g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs catalyst with visible photocatalytic activity was successfully synthesized.

The crystal structure of the material was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The Bruker D8 Advance was used with Cu target K α radiation (2θ = 20–80°) with a scanning speed of 5° min−1 and a scanning range of 10∼80°. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) is used to analyze the morphology of the material, and the accompanying X-ray energy spectrometer (EDS) is used to test the type and content of elements in the micro-regional composition of the material, as well as the analysis of the distribution of the elements using the analytical energy spectrum surface scan (mapping). The morphology of the material was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). One to two mg of sample was taken, dispersed into solution with ethanol, and tested by dropping into a 200/200 duplex square-hole copper mesh after sonication, with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was used to analyze the light absorption properties of the materials. By UV–vis spectrophotometer (PE950), BaSO4 was used as a blank substrate and scanned in the range of 200∼800 nm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was determined by CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd, China). The amplitude of the sinusoidal wave was 10 mV, and the frequency range from 0.05 Hz to 100 kHz.

The photocatalytic performance of the catalyst was evaluated by the removal rate of the target pollutant under visible light irradiation (500 W xenon lamp). 10 mg g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs photocatalyst was added into 50 mL, 10 mg L−1 of target pollutant solution, sonicated for 10 min to make it well dispersed, and then dark adsorbed for 30 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution was subjected to photocatalytic degradation under a xenon lamp, sampled at specific time intervals and filtered using 0.22 μm organic filter membranes. The residual target concentration was tested by UV–vis absorption spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-2450). The removal rate was calculated using Equation (1).
(1) Removal rate (%) = [(C 0 - C t)/C 0 ] × 100
where C 0 and C t (mg L−1) are the initial and residual concentration at time t, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of preparation conditions on g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs properties
ce:section.ce:para: The volume of CQDs solution added during the preparation of g-C3N4/CQDs was adjusted to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 mL to obtain g-C3N4/CQDs with different CQDs loadings. Obtained products were noted as g-C3N4 /n CQDs (n = 0.1 %, 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, n represents the mass ratio of CQDs to g-C3N4). The photocatalytic activity of the prepared g-C3N4/CQDs was evaluated by degradation of MB in visible light, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 . As can be seen from Fig. 1a, g-C3N4 shows weak adsorption and catalytic activity and only 27.5 % of MB is removed within 120 min. The g-C3N4 loaded with CQDs shows higher adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity than single g-C3N4, which can confirm that CQDs play a key role in the enhanced photocatalytic activity because of it's excellent electron transfer and light trapping ability. Among the materials obtained with different CQDs loadings, g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs shows the highest photocatalytic activity with 53.9 % removal of MB. g-C3N4/0.1 % CQDs, g-C3N4/1.0 % CQDs, and g-C3N4/1.5 % CQDs show 49.8 %, 48.3 %, and 46.3 % removal of MB, respectively. To compare the photodegradation activities of different catalysts, kinetic analysis was performed and the results are shown in Fig. 1b and c. The degradation rate of g-C3N4/CQDs is significantly faster than that of pure g-C3N4. As the loading of CQDs is increased from 0 to 0.5 %, the degradation rate constant is increased from 0.00212 min−1 to 0.00542 min−1. However, with further increase in the content of CQDs (0.5–1.5 %), the degradation rate is decreased significantly and the reaction rate is dropped to 0.00421 min−1. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that CQDs compete with g-C3N4 for the absorption of photons, thus reducing the formation of active substances [31].

Based on the optimal g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs, a series of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs ternary composite photocatalysts were further prepared. The total mass ratio of CaTiO3 in g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs was used as an indicator to adjust the contents of CaTiO3 to 10 %, 15 %, 20 % and 60 %, respectively. g-C3N4 /y CaTiO3/CQDs (y represents the content of CaTiO3) photocatalysts were named as different samples. Fig. 2 a shows the degradation effect of different g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs for MB. It is found that g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs shows the best degradation ability, with the MB removal rate up to 79.8 %. The addition of CaTiO3 could play a synergistic role with CQDs to promote the migration of photogenerated electrons, which jointly improve the photodegradation ability of the material. When the content of CaTiO3 is increased to 60 %, the photocatalytic activity is decreased significantly, which could be attributed to the excess CaTiO3 hindering the light absorption. The first-level kinetic fitting curves of the photocatalytic reaction are shown in Fig. 2b, and the kinetic constants (k) are shown in Fig. 2c. The degradation rate is the fastest when the CaTiO3 content is 15 %, and k is 0.00891 min−1.

Fig. 3 a shows the degradation effects of MB by CaTiO3, g-C3N4, g-C3N4/60 % CaTiO3, g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs, g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3 and g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs under visible light. As a comparison, the photodegradation of MB itself in the absence of photocatalyst is also tested. The results show that only 5.2 % of MB is degraded in the absence of photocatalyst. The single g-C3N4 and CaTiO3 photocatalytic activities are poor, with removal rates of only 27.5 % and 35.9 %. When g-C3N4, CaTiO3 and CQDs are formed binary or ternary complexes, the photocatalytic activities of the resulting composite photocatalysts are significantly enhanced. Among them, g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs ternary photocatalyst shows the best photocatalytic degradation of MB, with a removal rate of 79.8 % after 120 min under visible light irradiation. Fig. 3b shows the fitted degradation kinetic curves for each material, and their corresponding kinetic rate constants k are given in Fig. 3c g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs has the largest k value of 0.01168 min–1, which is about 8 times higher than that of g-C3N4 (0.00146 min-1), 5.5 times higher than that of CaTiO3 (0.00212 min-1), 4.6 times higher than that of g-C3N4/60 % CaTiO3 (0.00253 min-1), 2.2 times higher than that of g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs (0.00542 min-1) and 3.0 times higher than that of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3 (0.00388 min-1). The results proved that g-C3N4, CaTiO3 and CQDs have a synergistic role to promote the photodegradation ability of the composite material.

Fig. 4 shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of the prepared g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs, g-C3N4 and CaTiO3 photocatalysts. g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs diffraction peaks show no significant changes in position, peak shape and peak height compared with the XRD patterns of g-C3N4 [30], indicating that the introduction of CaTiO3 and CQDs does not affect the structure and the phase purity of g-C3N4. In addition, no typical diffraction peaks of CQDs are observed (2θ = 26°) [32], which may be due to the low content of CQDs, and a similar situation is observed in other studies [33,34]. In g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs diffraction peaks, the characteristic peaks at 26.5° correspond to the (002) crystal plane of typical g-C3N4 (PDF number 87-1526). And the characteristic peaks at 23.2, 33.1, 47.5 and 59.3° correspond to the (101), (121), (202) and (123) crystal planes of typical CaTiO3 (PDF number 42-0423). The above results suggest the synthesis of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs with high crystallinity.

Fig. 5 a shows the SEM images of g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs. The comparison shows that there is no significant difference in the morphology of g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs and pure g-C3N4 previously prepared [30], which are still blocky. Fig. 5b shows the SEM images of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs, which remains the spherical morphology of CaTiO3. In g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs, the g-C3N4 is not undergoed obvious thermal exfoliation. However, g-C3N4/0.5 % CQDs is split from large lumps to slightly smaller size lumps after calcination, which tightly surrounds CaTiO3. Fig. 5c and d show the elemental distribution images and EDS mapping images, respectively. It can be seen that Ca, Ti, O, C and N elements exist in g-C3N4/15%CaTiO3/CQDs, where Ca, Ti and O elements are uniformly distributed on the spherical CaTiO3, while C and N elements are uniformly dispersed around CaTiO3 as well as on the surface.

In order to determine the complexation of the CQDs, a more detailed microstructural characterization was performed using TEM. The morphology of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 6 a, where a uniform distribution of spherical CQDs with diameters less than 10 nm can be observed without obvious aggregation. TEM of g-C3N4/15%CaTiO3/CQDs (Fig. 6b) confirms that CQDs are evenly dispersed on the surface of g-C3N4, and CaTiO3 is in close contact with g-C3N4. This structure could effectively promote the charge separation and transfer and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs [35]. The above results show that CQDs are successfully loaded onto the g-C3N4 surface and a tight heterostructure is formed between g-C3N4/CQDs and CaTiO3.

The energy band structure is important for the analysis of the underlying causes of the enhanced photocatalytic activity. Fig. 7 shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance profiles of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/0.5%CQDs and g-C3N4/15%CaTiO3/CQDs. Compared with g-C3N4, the absorption wavelength edge of g-C3N4/0.5%CQDs is expanded from 456 nm to 475 nm with a red shift of about 19 nm (Fig. 7a). g-C3N4/15%CaTiO3/CQDs not only has a high photoresponse in the UV region, but also exhibites light absorption in the visible range with a maximum absorption. In Fig. 7b, the forbidden bandwidth of g-C3N4/0.5%CQDs is shortened from 2.80 eV to 2.78 eV for g-C3N4 by the Kubelka-Munk formula. The band gap width of CaTiO3 is also significantly shortened to 2.88 eV after compounding with g-C3N4/0.5%CQDs, which can greatly improve the utilization of visible light and is more favorable for photocatalysis.

To confirm the effect of modification on the electrochemical properties, the EIS analysis of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs, CaTiO3 and g-C3N4 was performed. The EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 8 ) reflect the interfacial charge transfer rate of the materials. The smaller the arc radius, the smaller the resistance. Obviously, the arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plots follow the order of g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs < CaTiO3 < g-C3N4, which proves that g-C3N4/15 % CaTiO3/CQDs has the smallest impedance and the best conductivity. The introduction of CQDs can enhance the electron transfer of the composites, which is the main reason for the improvement of carrier separation efficiency and photoelectrocatalytic degradation ability.

The initial pH of the solution plays an important role in the photocatalytic process. To further understand the effect of solution pH on the MLX degradation by g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs, the photocatalytic experiments were carried out at different pH conditions (Fig. 9 a). The removal of MLX is 97.6 %, 95.3 %, 98.7 %, 98.9 %, 99.7 % and 97.4 % when the pH is 3, 5, 7, unadjusted (pH ≈ 7.5), 9 and 11, respectively. The change in MLX removal rate with pH is small, but the adsorption of MLX in acidic media is much greater than that in neutral and basic media. Zeta potential analysis shows that the electronegativity of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs catalyst surface is decreased with increasing pH and has a stable negative potential at pH = 6.5 (Fig. 9b). Meloxicam has an acidity coefficient of pKa = 4.08 and can ionize cations [36]. Therefore, the negative charge on the surface of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs can adsorb MLX by electrostatic gravitation, which enhances the adsorption to some extent. Considering the practical application, the unadjusted pH is selected in the subsequent experiments.

The amount of catalyst dosage is one of the most important indicators affecting the removal of pollutants and the actual cost of use. In general, the removal rate is accelerated when the dosage is increased, but too much dosage can lead to material agglomeration, water turbidity, causing light scattering and affecting the utilization rate of the material [37]. Therefore, it is necessary to find a suitable dosage to make the right ratio of material and pollutant to provide sufficient surface area and sufficient photoactive sites. The effect of the dosage on the MLX degradation was investigated by varying the catalyst dosage in the range of 0–20 mg, and the results are shown in Fig. 9c. MLX is not degraded without catalyst. When the dosage of catalyst is increased from 5 mg to 10 mg, the removal rate of MLX is increased from 83.5 % to 98.9 % within 120 min. Continuing to increase the dosage of the material to 15 mg and 20 mg, the corresponding removal rates are 99.2 % and 98.0 %, respectively, with little change. In summary, the optimal dosage of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs is determined to be 10 mg from the consideration of pollutant removal rate and actual usage cost, and used for further experiments.

Actual wastewater usually contains several types of inorganic ions, which can accelerate or inhibit the degradation rate of the target by photocatalysts. Therefore, the resistance of the photocatalytic system to the anions was investigated by adding 2 mM NaCl, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, and Na3PO4 to the solution under the same other experimental conditions. As can be seen from Fig. 9d, the presence of Cl− has almost no effect on the photodegradation, and the removal rate of MLX remains at 98.1 %. However, in the presence of SO4 2−, CO3 2− and PO4 3−, the removal rates are decreased to 92.6 %, 95.8 % and 97.7 %, respectively. It is shown that the burst effect of inorganic anions on the reactive species produced in the advanced oxidation process (AOPs) is the main reason [38]. For CO3 2−, it can react with reactive species h+ and ⋅OH to form carbonate radicals (CO3⋅-). SO4 2− can also consume h+ and ⋅OH to form sulfate radicals (SO4⋅-). And for PO4 3−, it will react with ⋅OH to form phosphate radicals (PO4⋅2−) [39]. The above radicals are less active than ⋅OH and h+, resulting in poor degradation of MLX. The reaction processes are shown in Equations (2)–(6).
(2) CO3 2− + h+ → CO3⋅-
(3) CO3 2− +⋅OH → CO3⋅- + H2O
(4) SO4 2− + h+ → SO4⋅-.
(5) SO4 2− +⋅OH → OH− + SO4⋅-
(6) PO4 3− +⋅OH → PO4⋅2− + OH−

Humic acid (HA) is a typical dissolved organic carbon usually found in natural water environment. The presence of HA may have a significant negative effect on the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Fig. 9e shows the effects of different concentrations of HA on the MLX degradation. The results show that the removal rate of MLX is decreased significantly in both the dark adsorption and photocatalytic processes. The higher the concentration of HA, the more obvious the inhibition effect on the degradation of MLX. When the concentration of HA is increased from 0.5 mg L−1 to 4 mg L−1, the removal rate of MLX is decreased from 94.0 % to 80.1 %. The reason for this phenomenon may be that HA can cause light attenuation and reduce the available light reaching the catalyst surface. In addition, HA can trap photogenerated holes and reactive oxygen species [40], thus reducing the degradation efficiency of MLX.

In the study, MLX samples were configured with deionized water (DI water), tap water, river water (Hun River in Shenyang) and lake water (Bosong Lake in Shenyang Pharmaceutical University) as the solvents to evaluate the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs in different water matrices, respectively. From Fig. 9f, it can be seen that the removal rates of MLX in tap water, river water and lake water are 96.3 %, 90.5 % and 94.3 %, respectively, which are all slightly lower than the removal rate of 98.9 % in DI water. This may be due to the complexity of water matrices and the competition for active sites will occur between organic or inorganic substances and MLX. Another reason could be that the turbidity of water body will weaken the light reaching the catalyst surface. But overall, the material has good photocatalytic performance in different water bodies with the removal rate of over 90 %. This indicates g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs has excellent resistance to water quality interference and is an effective photocatalyst with good practicality in water purification.

To evaluate the reusability of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs, recycling experiments were performed. For each degradation experiment the photocatalysts were recovered by centrifugation, and then washed several times with methanol and distilled water and dried. The dried materials were applied in the next cyclic degradation experiment. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the removal rate of MLX by g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs could still reach 81.1 % after four cycles, indicating that the photocatalyst has good reusability and stability. The slight decrease in removal rate may be due to the loss of catalyst during the collection and washing process. In addition, a small amount of contaminants remained on the catalyst surface can reduce the number of available active sites, which also explains the decrease in the dark adsorption capacity of the catalyst after each cycle [41]. The photocatalysts are characterized by XRD before and after degradation. As can be seen from Fig. 10b, the diffraction peaks of the catalyst before and after degradation do not change significantly, indicating that g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs catalyst has good photocatalytic stability.

In the photocatalytic process, the active species usually include hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH), superoxide radicals (⋅O2 −), and holes (h+). In order to investigate the mechanism of photodegradation of MLX by g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs and identify the main active species of the reaction process, radical capture experiments were performed. Under the same other experimental conditions, isopropanol (IPA, 1 mM) and ammonium oxalate (AO, 1 mM) were added to the reaction system as ⋅OH and h+ trapping agents, respectively. N2 was continuously introduced into the solution to scavenge ⋅O2 −. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 11 . After the addition of IPA and AO, the removal rate of MLX is 87.9 % and 93.4 %, respectively, with slightly inhibition, indicating that the role of ⋅OH and h+ in the photocatalytic degradation process is weak. However, N2 can reduce the removal of MLX to 40.16 %, indicating that ⋅O2 − is the main active species in the photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, the contribution order of the three active species is ⋅O2 − > ⋅OH > h+.

Based on the above radical capture experimental results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs was proposed. As shown in Fig. 12 , the CB potential of CaTiO3 is higher than that of g-C3N4, but lower than the VB potential of g-C3N4, so a carrier transfer system is formed between CaTiO3 and g-C3N4. Fig. 12a shows the electron transfer pattern of a conventional type II heterojunction. Under visible light, both CaTiO3 and g-C3N4 can be excited to produce photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+). Electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 are transferred to the CB of CaTiO3, while holes of CaTiO3 are transferred to the VB of g-C3N4, thus achieving the separation of e− and h+. However, the CB potential of g-C3N4 is not negative enough compared to the standard redox potential of O2/⋅O2 − (−0.33 eV) [42]. The VB potential of CaTiO3 is not positive enough compared to the standard redox potential of H2O/⋅OH (1.99 eV) [43]. Therefore, O2 and H2O could not be converted to ⋅O2 − and ⋅OH. This is contrary to the results of the capture experiments. Therefore, the photocatalytic charge transfer does not follow the conventional type II heterojunction mechanism.

For this reason, a more reasonable Z-type charge transfer mechanism is proposed, as shown in Fig. 12b. Due to the upconversion property of CQDs, the light with λ > 475 nm can be converted to light with λ < 475 nm [44], which is subsequently absorbed by g-C3N4, improving the light utilization. e− in the CB of g-C3N4 migrates into the VB of CaTiO3 and then binds to h+. The CB of g-C3N4 and the VB of CaTiO3 with excellent redox potential will retain e− and h+, respectively, which can convert O2 and H2O to the strongly oxidizing ⋅O2 − and ⋅OH. This is consistent with the results of the capture experiments. CQDs can also act as a charge transfer bridge between g-C3N4 and CaTiO3, leading to further spatial separation of electrons. Due to the synergistic effect of the above processes, the g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs catalyst exhibit high redox potential and excellent charge carrier separation, thus enhancing the photocatalytic degradation performance.

The degradation products of meloxicam were analyzed by electrospray electrostatic field orbital trap high-resolution mass spectrometry in positive ion mode (ESI+). The mass spectra are shown in Fig. S1. Nine intermediate compounds were analyzed in combination with the mass spectra and the details are recorded in Table 1 . Based on the obtained product structures, the possible degradation pathways of MLX were postulated as shown in Fig. 13 . The initial substrate MLX is ionized in electrospray mass spectrometry to obtain the protonated excimer ion peak at m/z 352. The first degradation pathway exhibits the breakage of the C–N bond of MLX by free radical attack to form intermediates P1 (m/z 239) and P2 (m/z 115). P2 (C4H6N2S) is 2-amino-5-methylthiazole, one of the main raw materials for the synthesis of MLX. P2 is then further oxidized and ring-opened to give P3 (m/z 89). And the N–S bond of P1 is attacked and broken to form P7 (m/z 227), which then undergoes a series of oxidations to form the product P9 (m/z 82). The second pathway is that the pentacyclic enol structure of MLX is oxidatived ring opening and the carbonyl and methyl groups are deacylated to give the product P4 (m/z 158). The third pathway, P5 (m/z 170) is formed due to the ring-breaking and demethylation of the pentacyclic enol structure of MLX, and then the bond is broken again by oxidation to produce P6 (m/z 58). The final obtained small molecular compounds can be mineralized into H2O, CO2, SO4 2−, NH4 +, etc.

In summary, Z-type heterojunction photocatalyst g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs was successfully prepared and used to degrade organic pollutants in water under the visible light. Due to the upconversion property of CQDs, the light utilization of the heterojunction catalyst is improved. CQDs also act as the charge transfer bridge between g-C3N4 and CaTiO3, leading to the effective charge carrier separation and excellent photocatalytic degradation performance. In the process of MLX degradation by g-C3N4/CaTiO3/CQDs, O2 − is the main active species, and the contribution order of the active substances is ⋅O2 − > ⋅OH > h+. The possible degradation pathways of MLX were also analyzed. This study will help to establish Z-type heterostructure to enhance photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in water.

Jiamei Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Qiangyi Deng: Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Ziyin Yang: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hongmei Zhang: Supervision. Conglu Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Fund Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province (LJKZ0910) and the Young and Middle-aged Key Talent Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2021026).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of N-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4 photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity for the degradation of indomethacin
Appl. Catal. B Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.L. Wang, P. Chen, Y.P. Feng, Z.J. Xie, Y. Liu, Y.H. Su, Q.X. Zhang, Y.F. Wang, K. Yao, W.Y. Lv, G.G. Liu, Facile synthesis of N-doped carbon dots/g-C3N4 photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity for the degradation of indomethacin, Appl. Catal. B-Environ. 207 (2017) 103-113.
